text: The task-positive network does not act alone. A third system — the salience network, anchored by the anterior insula and the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex — acts as a switch between the default and task-positive networks. Neuroscientist Vinod Menon describes it as the brain's relevance detector: it continuously monitors internal and external signals and decides which large-scale network should be in command. When the salience network malfunctions — as in certain anxiety disorders — it may fail to suppress the default mode network even when focused work demands otherwise, leaving people trapped in ruminative loops that persist despite their best intentions to concentrate.
question: What role does Vinod Menon assign to the salience network?

options.text: It encodes long-term memories during focused tasks
It boosts the task-positive network's processing speed
It acts as a switch between the default and task-positive networks
It suppresses emotional processing during high-demand tasks
